title: Prime Minister of Sweden

infobox.value: Mister Prime Minister ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): Herr Statsminister) (informal) [His Excellency](/wiki/Excellency "Excellency") (diplomatic)

* [Government of Sweden](/wiki/Government_of_Sweden "Government of Sweden") * [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") * [National Security Council](/wiki/National_Security_Council_(Sweden) "National Security Council (Sweden)")
[Louis Gerhard De Geer](/wiki/Louis_Gerhard_De_Geer "Louis Gerhard De Geer")

**of Sweden** ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *statsminister*, lit. "minister of state") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") of the [Kingdom of Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"). The prime minister and their cabinet (the government) exercise executive authority in the Kingdom of Sweden and are subject to the [Parliament of Sweden](/wiki/Parliament_of_Sweden "Parliament of Sweden"). The prime minister is nominated by the [speaker of the Riksdag](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Riksdag "Speaker of the Riksdag") and is elected by the chamber by simple majority, using negative parliamentarianism. The Riksdag [holds elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Sweden "Elections in Sweden") every four years, in the even year between leap years.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The **prime minister** **of Sweden** ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *statsminister*, lit. "minister of state") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") of the [Kingdom of Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"). The prime minister and their cabinet (the government) exercise executive authority in the Kingdom of Sweden and are subject to the [Parliament of Sweden](/wiki/Parliament_of_Sweden "Parliament of Sweden"). The prime minister is nominated by the [speaker of the Riksdag](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Riksdag "Speaker of the Riksdag") and is elected by the chamber by simple majority, using negative parliamentarianism. The Riksdag [holds elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Sweden "Elections in Sweden") every four years, in the even year between leap years.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

As with several other similar offices in Europe, the office of Prime Minister came into existence in the nineteenth century as a result of Sweden's democratisation. Prior to the creation of the office, Sweden had no official head of government separate from the king; the country in periods was an absolute monarchy. However, several figures had formerly attained *de facto* status as leader of the government. Today, the prime minister holds the most influential political role in Sweden.

Unlike most prime ministers in parliamentary systems, the prime minister is both *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* and *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* chief executive. This is because the [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Basic_Laws_of_Sweden#Instrument_of_Government "Basic Laws of Sweden") explicitly vests executive power in the [government](/wiki/Government_of_Sweden "Government of Sweden"), of which the prime minister is the leader. The prime minister has two official residences; these are the [Sager House](/wiki/Sager_House "Sager House") and [Harpsund](/wiki/Harpsund "Harpsund").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_Minister_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Historically, the [monarchy of Sweden](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Sweden "Monarchy of Sweden") served as both head of state and head of government. Examples like Kings [Gustav I](/wiki/Gustav_I_of_Sweden "Gustav I of Sweden"), [Charles XI](/wiki/Charles_XI_of_Sweden "Charles XI of Sweden"), and [Gustav III](/wiki/Gustav_III "Gustav III") showcase how the Swedish government was structured around the monarchy. However, many of these kings had powerful domestic advisors who sometimes took on the role of *de facto* head of government; the most prominent of these examples is [Axel Oxenstierna](/wiki/Axel_Oxenstierna "Axel Oxenstierna"), who played a pivotal role in the formation of the [Swedish Empire](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire"). The office of [Lord High Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_High_Chancellor_of_Sweden "Lord High Chancellor of Sweden") was commonly the closest role to a *de jure* head of government, and they had similar responsibilities to the modern Prime Minister during the so-called [Age of Liberty](/wiki/Age_of_Liberty "Age of Liberty"); no governmental offices were called Prime Minister at the time.

At the adoption of the new [Instrument of Government of 1809](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government_(1809) "Instrument of Government (1809)"), the two offices of **Prime Minister for Justice** ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *justitiestatsminister*) and **Prime Minister for Foreign Affairs** ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *utrikesstatsminister*) were created, though their roles were no more than just the heads of their respective ministries. When the office of the prime minister was created in 1876, the prime ministers for justice and foreign affairs were thus subsequently demoted to [Minister for Justice](/wiki/Minister_for_Justice_(Sweden) "Minister for Justice (Sweden)") and [Minister for Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Minister_for_Foreign_Affairs_(Sweden) "Minister for Foreign Affairs (Sweden)"). Unlike the minister for justice, the minister for foreign affairs did, however, continue to be [styled](/wiki/Style_(manner_of_address) "Style (manner of address)") as "[Excellency](/wiki/Excellency "Excellency")", an honour shared only with the prime minister.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)
After 1917, it was no longer possible for a monarch to appoint the prime minister and the councillors of state (cabinet ministers) at their own discretion, or keep them in office against the will of the Riksdag.[[5]](#cite_note-5) From that time onward, while the king still formally appointed the prime minister, in practice he was required to appoint the leader of the majority party in the Riksdag, or the leader of the senior partner in the majority coalition. While the provision in the Instrument of Government stating that "the King alone shall govern the realm" remained unchanged, it was now understood that the king was required to exercise his powers through the ministers and act on their advice. Over time, the ministers came to *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* exercise the [royal prerogatives](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"). However, the Swedish term used for the government during this period was still *Kungl. Maj:t*, an abbreviation of *Kunglig Majestät* 'Royal Majesty'.

Until 1974, the executive authority in Sweden had been exercised through the [King in Council](/wiki/King_in_Council_(Sweden) "King in Council (Sweden)"). [Constitutional](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") reform provided a new [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Constitution_of_Sweden "Constitution of Sweden") which *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* established the [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") and created a [cabinet government](/wiki/Cabinet_government "Cabinet government") with constitutional powers not derived from the Crown. At the same time, it stripped the monarchy of even nominal political powers, making the cabinet the country's executive authority in both name and in fact. This codified a number of practices dating from the definitive establishment of parliamentary government in 1917.

## Duties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_Minister_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Duties")]

See also: [Politics of Sweden](/wiki/Politics_of_Sweden "Politics of Sweden")

The [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government_(Sweden) "Instrument of Government (Sweden)") requires that the prime minister appoint a member of the cabinet as [Deputy Prime Minister](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_Sweden "Deputy Prime Minister of Sweden"), to perform the duties of the prime minister if the prime minister cannot. However, if a deputy prime minister is absent or has not been appointed, the senior minister in the cabinet becomes acting head of government. If more than one minister has equal tenure, the eldest assumes the position (see *[Swedish governmental line of succession](/wiki/Swedish_governmental_line_of_succession "Swedish governmental line of succession")* for the present governmental line of succession).

Constitutionally, the prime minister's position is stronger than that of their counterparts in [Denmark](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Denmark "Prime Minister of Denmark") and [Norway](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Norway "Prime Minister of Norway"). Since 1975, the prime minister has been both *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* and *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* chief executive, with powers and duties specifically enumerated in the Instrument of Government. In the two neighboring Scandinavian monarchies, the monarch is the nominal chief executive, but is bound by convention to act on the advice of the ministers. However, the so-called *Torekov Compromise* reached in 1971 by the major political parties, codified with the Instrument of Government that went into effect in 1975, stripped the [Swedish monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Sweden "Monarchy of Sweden") of even a nominal role in governmental affairs, thus codifying actual practices that had been in place since the definitive establishment of parliamentary government in 1917.

## Process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_Minister_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Process")]

### Appointment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_Minister_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Appointment")]

To appoint a new prime minister, the [speaker of the Riksdag](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Riksdag "Speaker of the Riksdag") holds consultations with party leaders to propose a candidate to be submitted for approval to the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The speaker's proposed candidate is then elected through negative parliamentarism. In practice, this means that the prime minister nominee is confirmed if fewer than 175 MPs vote 'no', regardless of the number of 'yes' votes or abstentions.[[7]](#cite_note-7) This is described as being "tolerated" by a majority of the Riksdag.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

After approval by the Riksdag, the new [prime minister-designate](/wiki/Prime_minister-designate "Prime minister-designate") must inform the Riksdag which ministers are chosen to make up the new government.

The formal change of government, and thus the start of the term for the new prime minister takes place at a Council of State at the [Royal Palace](/wiki/Stockholm_Palace "Stockholm Palace"). This is a government meeting chaired by the [king](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Sweden "Monarchy of Sweden"), currently [Carl XVI Gustaf](/wiki/Carl_XVI_Gustaf "Carl XVI Gustaf"). During this meeting, the speaker gives an account of the nomination and election process. The King then announces that a change of government has taken place, finalising the appointment of the new prime minister and their government.[[9]](#cite_note-Forming-9) The handover of the prime minister's [commission](/wiki/Commission_(document) "Commission (document)") has usually taken place in the Riksdag before the Council of State. In 2022 with the appointment of Ulf Kristersson, however, speaker [Andreas Norlén](/wiki/Andreas_Norl%C3%A9n "Andreas Norlén") handed it over during the meeting of the Council of State after asking for and receiving the Kings approval to do so, a pure formality.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Resignation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_Minister_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Resignation")]

Whenever a prime minister resigns, dies, or is forced from office by the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag"), the [speaker of the Riksdag](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Riksdag "Speaker of the Riksdag") asks the prime minister (or their deputy) to keep the government as a [caretaker government](/wiki/Caretaker_government "Caretaker government") until the new government takes office.[[9]](#cite_note-Forming-9)

With the exception of the prime minister, cabinet ministers ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *statsråd* [[ˈstatsroːd]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Swedish "Help:IPA/Swedish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:SV_-_Statsr%C3%A5d.ogg "File:SV - Statsråd.ogg")) do not need the approval of the Riksdag, but they can be forced to resign by a vote of no confidence.[[11]](#cite_note-11) If the prime minister is forced by a vote of no confidence to resign, the entire cabinet falls, and the process of electing a new prime minister starts. The prime minister can dissolve the Riksdag, even after receiving a vote of no confidence, except during the first three months after an election.[[9]](#cite_note-Forming-9)

## Amenities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_Minister_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Amenities")]

### Office and residences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_Minister_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Office and residences")]

The government offices, including the prime minister's office, are located at [Rosenbad](/wiki/Rosenbad "Rosenbad") in central [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), straight across the water from the [Riksdag building](/wiki/Riksdag_building "Riksdag building") on [Helgeandsholmen](/wiki/Helgeandsholmen "Helgeandsholmen").

In 1991 [Sager House](/wiki/Sager_House "Sager House") (or the "Sager Palace" as it was previously called) was acquired, and since 1995 it has served as the private residence of the prime minister.

[Harpsund](/wiki/Harpsund "Harpsund"), a [manor house](/wiki/Manor_house "Manor house") in [Flen Municipality](/wiki/Flen_Municipality "Flen Municipality"), [Södermanland County](/wiki/S%C3%B6dermanland_County "Södermanland County"), has served as a country residence for the prime minister since 1953. The manor is also frequently used for governmental conferences and informal summits between the government, industry and organisations in Sweden.

### Salary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_Minister_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Salary")]

The salaries of the cabinet ministers, including the prime minister, is decided by and is the subject of annual review by the *Statsrådsarvodesnämnden* 'Cabinet Ministers' Salary Committee' of the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag"). Since 1 January 2022 the prime minister's monthly salary is 184,000 [SEK](/wiki/Swedish_krona "Swedish krona").[[12]](#cite_note-salary-12)
